candidates.quote: The Clubfoot was a traitor as well, he decided. How else would Daemon and Rhaenyra have known when King’s Landing was most vulnerable? Someone on the small council had sent word to them...
candidates.note: Aemond's in-world suspicion (voiced during his Harrenhal reprisal) that Larys Strong fed the blacks intelligence enabling the fall of King's Landing; never confirmed on-page.

candidates.quote: the haste with which the castellan had yielded Harrenhal to Daemon Targaryen convinced him the old man was a traitor.
candidates.note: Aemond suspects Ser Simon Strong of treasonously surrendering Harrenhal to Daemon without a fight; Simon protests innocence and the narrative leaves it genuinely ambiguous (he had no way to hold against a dragon) before Aemond executes him by trial-by-combat verdict, not proof.

candidates.quote: the widows of Myr and Tyrosh accused the admiral of sending their fleets to destruction whilst holding back his own
candidates.note: Myrish/Tyroshi widows accuse Sharako Lohar of deliberately sacrificing allied ships while sparing his own Lysene squadron during the Gullet; presented as accusation/grievance, not confirmed fact.

candidates.quote: but Vance and Piper and their ilk were more like to help the Blackfish escape than clap him into fetters.
candidates.note: Jaime's own reasoning for excluding the riverlords from the hunt for Brynden Tully — he suspects Karyl Vance (and Clement Piper) would shelter the Blackfish rather than catch him. Node prose already flagged this exact SUSPECTED_OF pairing as un-minted; this candidate mints it with book grounding.

candidates.quote: but Vance and Piper and their ilk were more like to help the Blackfish escape than clap him into fetters.
candidates.note: Same passage as S4 — "Piper" here is Lord Clement Piper (per node's own prior identification of the pairing). Jaime's unproven suspicion that Piper would abet Brynden Tully's escape rather than help catch him.

candidates.quote: Ser Morgan Hightower did the deed at the command of his lord brother, some say (and Ser Morgan was seen entering and leaving the High Septon’s privy chambers that night).
candidates.note: One of several rival theories of the High Septon's death during Maegor's siege of Oldtown; text explicitly frames it as unproven ("some say") alongside competing suspects.

candidates.quote: Others point to the Lady Patrice Hightower, Lord Martyn’s maiden aunt and a reputed witch (who did indeed seek an audience with His High Holiness at dusk, though he was alive when she departed).
candidates.note: A second rival theory in the same passage; text notes she did visit the High Septon that evening but he was alive when she left, leaving her culpability explicitly unresolved.

candidates.quote: Ser Morgan Hightower did the deed at the command of his lord brother, some say
candidates.note: The theory naming Morgan Hightower as the killer also implicates his "lord brother" (Lord Martyn Hightower, Lord of Oldtown) as the one who ordered it — a nested, doubly-unproven suspicion voiced in the same sentence.

candidates.quote:
there are still others who hold them all blameless and lay the High

Septon’s death at the door of another rumored sorceress, the Dowager Queen Visenya Targaryen.
candidates.note: The fourth rival theory in the same debated-death passage (matches the node's own Identity summary, "rumor laid his convenient passing at Queen Visenya's door, though nothing was proven"). Quote spans the chapter's own line-wrap.

candidates.quote:
Helaena had been murdered, the whispers went, as her sons had been before her. Prince Daeron and his dragons would soon be at the gates, and with them the end of Rhaenyra’s reign. The old queen was determined that her young half-sister should not live to revel in her downfall, so she had sent Ser Luthor Largent to seize

Helaena with his huge rough hands and fling her from the window onto the spikes below.
candidates.note: The city-wide rumor (which ignites the King's Landing riot) that Rhaenyra ordered Helaena's murder. The very next paragraph in the same chapter states the rumor is almost certainly false and gives Ser Luthor Largent's alibi — SUSPECTED_OF captures the in-world suspicion itself, not a truth claim.

candidates.quote:
so she had sent Ser Luthor Largent to seize

Helaena with his huge rough hands and fling her from the window onto the spikes below.
candidates.note: Same rumor as S10, naming Luthor Largent as the purported hands-on killer. The chapter explicitly refutes this a few lines later with his barracks alibi ("we have abundant proof that Ser Luthor Largent, the purported killer, was eating with three hundred of his gold cloaks at the barracks") — a textbook false-rumor SUSPECTED_OF, not an assertion of fact.

candidates.quote: Euron is known to keep wizards and foul sorcerers on that red ship of his. They sent some spell among us, so we could not hear the sea.
candidates.note: Aeron Damphair's accusation to Victarion that Euron used sorcery to sway/rig the kingsmoot vote in his favor — an unproven in-world accusation (the node's own Foreshadowing section already treats Euron's sorcery as gated-until-TWoW), distinct from the existing euron-greyjoy+death-of-balon-greyjoy pair.

wiki_only.quote: It was rumored that there had been a "shadow host", who had been providing Walter with the funds to pay the magnificent prizes which were promised. These rumors were widely believed in 281 AC, and are still believed at the present. Whilst a dozen names have been put forward for the identity of this shadow host, Prince Rhaegar Targaryen is the most compelling. Rhaegar allegedly arranged the tourney by sending Ser Oswell Whent, his friend, and Lord Walter's brother, as a go-between.
wiki_only.note: The "shadow host" funding theory for the tourney at Harrenhal is TWOIAF-sourced (Great Council retrospective); TWOIAF is not one of the book ∈ {agot,acok,asos,affc,adwd,tmk,tss,thk,fab} sources available to Grep, and no equivalent scene surfaced in the split book chapters searched (AGOT/ASOS/AFFC Jaime and Ned material touching Harrenhal). Routed to wiki_only per the grounding rule rather than forced into candidates.

flags: death-of-aegon-ii: fab-hour-of-the-wolf-20.md (lines ~183-201) shows Cregan Stark's tribunal treating Larys Strong's guilt as established via Grand Maester Orwyle's testimony ("Orwyle had testified that he gave the poison to his lordship") and Corlys Velaryon OUTRIGHT CONFESSES ("What I did, I did for the good of the realm... I would do the same again"). This is materially stronger than plain suspicion. The existing larys-strong SUSPECTED_OF death-of-aegon-ii edge may be under-modeling what the text supports (arguably AGENT_IN/KILLS territory per honesty rule 2), and Corlys Velaryon's confessed complicity in the same poisoning isn't modeled on that event at all yet. Flagging for a follow-up pass rather than proposing corlys-velaryon SUSPECTED_OF here, since the text proves rather than merely suspects his involvement — proposing SUSPECTED_OF for a confessed act would violate this dip's own honesty rule.

battle-at-rooks-rest: Corlys Velaryon 'blamed [Rhaenys's] death on Queen Rhaenyra Targaryen, stating that if the queen or her sons had gone with their dragons, his wife might be alive.' Considered and rejected as a SUSPECTED_OF candidate — this is grief/blame for a negligent decision (not sending reinforcements), not suspicion of a concealed act Rhaenyra is alleged to have committed. Doesn't fit the 'agent/cause of an unproven event' shape; flagging in case a future BLAMES-style edge type is ever added.

faith-militant-uprising / assassination-of-septon-moon: the packet's wiki passage says 'many suspect Moon's death was ordered by the Hightowers but accounts differ,' but the actual Fire & Blood narration of the killing (fab-prince-into-king-06.md, lines ~123-129) shows Moon killed by an unidentified woman during a tryst, with the text stating 'his own guards could not even agree on what his killer had looked like' and no specific named Hightower conspirator anywhere in the surrounding pages. No named individual suspect could be grounded in book text, so per rule 3 (no named suspect = emit nothing) this yielded no candidate — 'the Hightowers' as a house-level rumor isn't a mintable SUSPECTED_OF source.

death-of-queen-ceryse: node prose calls this 'died; rumored murder,' yet existing_edges already carries AGENT_IN:maegor-i-targaryen (proven agency) for the same event rather than SUSPECTED_OF. Possible schema mismatch worth a orchestrator/schema-drift look — either the node prose undersells the confidence or the edge overclaims it.

battle-of-oxcross: Lancel Lannister's 'vile sorcery' propaganda about Robb Stark's tactics is not a whodunit suspicion (Robb's COMMANDS_IN role in this battle is already proven/modeled) — it's a rumor about method, not agency, so no SUSPECTED_OF candidate was minted.
